publications.editedComment: Riddle me this: when is a theme both a string and a room full of values? A built-in string becomes a DaisyUI preview; a custom record exposes name, tagline, visibility, dark preference, room, scheme, owner, id, and five color swatches. Editing belongs only to an administrator or owner. Copy serializes the safely parsed values. Apply sends either the built-in name or a normalized custom payload to the theme store, then emits the applied name and snapshot. Errors remain on the card. The apron has pockets for presentation, permission, clipboard, and activation—and each pocket is labeled.

We hold built-in and custom themes in one shared card without pretending they are identical. Built-ins carry their DaisyUI name; custom themes add public/private status, dark preference, room, scheme, owner, id, and parsed color values. The swatch row projects primary, secondary, accent, base, and content colors. Applying delegates to `themeStore.setActiveTheme`, reports success or error, and emits an accountable snapshot. Copy writes only the normalized value map. Edit is limited to administrators or the theme owner and loads the record into the shared form. Many palettes, one explicit path for who may change them.

Count the conditions carefully. Clicking the article emits one `select`. The actions slot stops its own clicks. A reaction button appears only when reactions are enabled, the card is selected, and target id, type, and category normalize into valid values. That button toggles a contained reaction panel; its close button closes it, and a submitted reaction closes it too. The panel receives normalized target identity and an Untitled fallback. If identity later becomes invalid, a watcher shuts the panel. Compact spacing, selected styling, and caller-supplied card classes alter presentation, not the contract. Eleven fingers; still only one selection event.

Here’s the house rule: the whole card selects, but the action tray does not spill its clicks into selection. Reactions are served only when the card is selected and its target id, target type, and category are all valid. Then the star opens a scrollable reaction-card with a normalized title; Close or a successful submission puts the panel away. If the target becomes invalid, the watcher closes the tab automatically. Compact, selected, and custom card classes change the glassware, not the drink. One card, one select event, and no reaction form poured for an unidentified target.

Admin-only x-ray goggles! :) The fixed button toggles a Teleported overlay showing viewport dimensions, orientation, touch/mobile flags, CSS `--vh`, display mode, action, route, main Component, and SmartState. Colored outlines measure named DOM regions and report width, height, and coordinates. The lower panel lists store states, missing selectors, raw snapshots, and controls for sidebars, header, footer, tutorial, extended view, corner, fullscreen, random animation, stop, and smart flip. Refresh remeasures; Apply viewport calls the store. Resize and orientation changes keep the readout current. Debugging stays visible, reversible, and reserved for administrators. Have a productive forever!

The overlay reflects both what the display store believes and what the DOM actually occupies. Administrators can open it to see viewport facts, device and orientation flags, layout section states, animation state, mode, action, SmartState, initialization, and CSS `--vh`. Measured regions are drawn over the page with live dimensions and coordinates; absent selectors receive their own list instead of invented boxes. Controls can toggle major display regions, fullscreen, tutorial, extended and corner states, random animation, and smart flip. Refresh measures again. The mirror does not repair layout; it makes disagreement between store and geometry impossible to hide.

reviewer.name: The Customer Who Buys One of Everything

publications.editedComment: Everything present is counted, not priced. The card lists each stored item’s image, type, ArtImage id, and editable quantity. Its local quantity map begins from the cart’s current items and sends changes to `cartStore.updateQuantity`; Remove deletes one item, Clear All clears the store, and Total Items comes from the store. Proceed to Checkout does not purchase anything: it logs the current item payload and shows a browser alert saying checkout is pretend. The empty cart has its own message. One of everything may enter this prototype, but none of it crosses a real payment boundary.

Darling, this cart knows how to pose, but it is not yet taking your card. It displays stored images, product types, ArtImage ids, quantities, removal controls, a Clear All action, and the store’s total item count. Quantity edits call the cart store directly. The dramatic Proceed to Checkout button merely prints the item payload to the console and raises a pretend-checkout alert. No Stripe call, order record, or payment request appears here. That distinction is the useful glamour: inventory interaction is real, checkout is rehearsal, and the empty-state copy tells you when the stage has cleared.

The committee has approved fifty-five members. Each node begins at a random canvas position with a small velocity, radius, and alpha. Every animation frame moves the nodes, reverses velocity at viewport boundaries, draws each point, then examines every later pair. Pairs closer than 160 pixels receive a line whose opacity falls with distance. Resize makes the canvas match the window. The full-screen canvas ignores pointer input and sits at z-index 50. On unmount, the scheduled frame is cancelled and the resize listener is removed. The stars are not semantic data; they are a bounded moving graph with recorded adjacencies.
